title: Speeding up cache hits with the --layers option
body:
**Description**

When rebuilding a particular image using the `--layers` option, each cache hit takes more than a second. Rebuilding that image can take about 18 seconds to complete. Docker does the same for that image in about 0.2 seconds.

I suggest adding an internal data structure that maps previous hash and next command pairs to the next hash. If there is a hit, buildah would simply continue on to the next cache lookup.

One advantage to speeding up cache hits is that it would be okay to add an image rebuild step to the beginning of a script that also launches containers after the rebuild step. If cache hits were fast, that would be great.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Create these files:

`base`:
````
FROM registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:31

RUN dnf -y update && \
    dnf -y install findutils gcc git libgit2-devel python3-alembic \
                   python3-arrow python3-bcrypt python3-beautifulsoup4 \
                   python3-binaryornot python3-black python3-bleach \
                   python3-blinker python3-celery python3-chardet \
                   python3-coverage python3-cryptography python3-devel \
                   python3-docutils python3-eventlet python3-fedmsg \
                   python3-fedora python3-fedora-flask python3-filelock \
                   python3-flake8 python3-flask python3-flask-oidc \
                   python3-flask-wtf python3-funcsigs python3-jinja2 \
                   python3-kitchen python3-markdown python3-mock \
                   python3-munch python3-nose python3-nose-xcover \
                   python3-openid python3-openid-cla python3-openid-teams \
                   python3-pillow python3-psutil python3-psycopg2 \
                   python3-pygit2 python3-redis python3-requests \
                   python3-setuptools python3-six python3-sqlalchemy \
                   python3-straight-plugin python3-trololio \
                   python-unversioned-command python3-wtforms which && \
    dnf clean all
````

`slow` (with arbitrary commands):
````
FROM test-base:dev

VOLUME ["/data"]
RUN mkdir /code
WORKDIR /code

ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/bash"]

EXPOSE 5000

VOLUME ["/code"]
````

Then build the images for the first time:

````
buildah bud --layers -t test-base:dev -f base
buildah bud --layers -t test-slow:dev -f slow
````

Now see how long it takes to hit all of the caches when rebuilding the image:

````
time buildah bud --layers -t test-slow:dev -f slow
````

**Describe the results you received:**

The rebuild of the slow container file takes about 18 - 28 seconds to complete.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Docker does this in about 0.2 seconds. It would be great if buildah was much faster.

Thanks! : )

comments.body: @sudoman thanks for the report.  @nalind has been toiling away at performance improvements for the build.  I've assigned this issue to him in case this aspect is not on his list.  (I think it is).

Hi, I tested out `buildah` from the `master` branch, and it still takes ~28 seconds to run this command:

````
$ time ../../cont/buildah/bin/buildah bud --layers -t test-slow:dev -f slow
...
real	0m27.637s
user	0m6.235s
sys	0m15.189s
````

Thanks!

```
time buildah bud --layers=true /tmp
STEP 1: FROM test-base:dev
STEP 2: VOLUME ["/data"]
--> Using cache 02b170f23197298d5d5cb2b070c37430e1b3fe0cd709895384889bc219fb118d
--> 02b170f2319
STEP 3: RUN mkdir -p /code
--> Using cache cd5a5dd3faa1be14be95bb98f49cdaf6986195dacf90c2f68dc2561033e3e227
--> cd5a5dd3faa
STEP 4: WORKDIR /code
--> Using cache 8420b9890dfc083b04b762d28c049b9a43af89f16139a854619cfbb2f255ec91
--> 8420b9890df
STEP 5: ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/bash"]
--> Using cache e16251983e0646c765149e48b3b2ae0b3b2a353b001e2be8e12cda892a235d20
--> e16251983e0
STEP 6: EXPOSE 5000
--> Using cache 31ca6b3d1b11f990e69cf66a99f8e73a88e33636a4cffa6d2daf70695f20d225
--> 31ca6b3d1b1
STEP 7: VOLUME ["/code"]
--> Using cache d3f5022440406338bb18a19437881859a31cb30b4d1af11b2cfb25a19cde16c9
--> d3f50224404
d3f5022440406338bb18a19437881859a31cb30b4d1af11b2cfb25a19cde16c9

real	0m0.546s
user	0m0.374s
sys	0m0.313s
```
Seems pretty quick to me.
